calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing On Motion And Motion For (1) Entry Of Judgment And (2) Award Of Attorneys Fees And Statutory Costs
Off Calendar May-09-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
Defendant Bank of San Francisco' motion for entry of judgment and for an award of attorneys' fees and statutory costs is denied in its entirety. The court lacks jurisdiction to enter a judgment in the Bank's favor because plaintiff Cornerstone Law Group voluntarily dismissed this case. (Viejo Bancorp, Inc. v. Wood (1989) 217 Cal.App.3d 200, 206). The Bank's request for fees is denied because the parties' dispute has now moved to federal court and thus, as a practical matter, it is not possible to determine which party, if either, has obtained its litigation objectives and thus can fairly be determined to be the "prevailing party." (Santisas v. Goodin (1998) 17 Cal.4th 599, 622). Order signed. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen CSR #8189 =302/hk

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-14-2017 Off Calendar

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-22-2017 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, John C. Brown of the law offices of Cornerstone Law Group (415.409.8600) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Michael C. Starkey of the law offices of French Lyon Tang (415.597.7816) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT BANK OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT as to the entirety of the sixth cause of action in the complaint filed by Cornerstone Law Group is off calendar in light of the ruling on the Bank's demurrer to the sixth cause of action.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org. (D302)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-22-2017 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, John C. Brown of the law offices of Cornerstone Law Group (415.409.8600) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Michael C. Starkey of the law offices of French Lyon Tang (415.597.7816) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT BANK OF SAN FRANCISCO DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT as to all but the second cause of action in the complaint filed by Cornerstone Law Group is SUSTAINED with twenty days leave to amend as to the first, third, fourth, and fifth causes of action and sustained without leave to amend as to the sixth, seventh and eighth causes of action. The first cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is insufficiently alleged per Holcomb v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (2007) 155 Cal. App. 4th 490, because there is no allegation that the Bank made an affirmative misrepresentation or that the Bank had received a notice of dishonor from the payor bank at the time it made the wire transfer to Cambodia. The third through fifth causes of action for negligence are insufficiently alleged because the alleged duties that were allegedly breached by the Bank are inconsistent with Holcomb and thus cannot serve as predicates for negligence claims. If it can do so in good faith, Cornerstone is granted leave to allege one or more viable claims for negligent misrepresentation and negligence that are not precluded by either the language or logic of Holcomb. The sixth cause of action fails as a matter of law and cannot reasonably be amended to state a viable claim because under the facts of this case a claim for negligent hiring is entirely duplicative of a claim for negligence. The additional allegation of negligent hiring adds nothing to a claim for negligence, even assuming that Cornerstone is able to allege a viable claim for negligence. The seventh cause of action for money had and received fails as a matter of law because Cornerstone has not alleged, nor does it appear reasonably possible that it can allege, that the cashier's check received by the Bank was not used as Cornerstone directed. Moreover, it does not appear that the Bank actually received any money as a result of the cashier's check, so that there was no money had or received by the Bank to support the seventh cause of action. The eighth cause of action for misleading partial disclosure fails as a matter of law because it is entirely duplicative of a claim for negligent misrepresentation. The additional allegation of partial disclosure adds nothing to a claim for negligent misrepresentation, even assuming that Cornerstone is able to allege a viable claim for negligent misrepresentation. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org. (D302)
